title: String Diagrams for Quantum Foundations, Computing and Natural Language Processing
abstract: Applied category theory provides powerful mathematical tools for modelling processes and their composition. Symmetric monoidal categories, which involve series and parallel composition, are particularly well-suited for describing the composition of processes in space and time. Also called process theories, they admit string diagrams, which constitute a visually intuitive, mathematically rigorous, expressive and flexible syntax that is applicable to wide-ranging scientific domains. In this thesis, we employ string diagrams to investigate a selection of topics in the areas of quantum foundations, computing, and natural language processing: (1) We formalise constructor theory as a process theory. In the context of quantum physics, we also demonstrate the conflict between constructor-theoretic principles of locality and composition. Moreover, we argue that if the principle of locality is rejected, categorical quantum mechanics (CQM) can be conceived as a constructor theory of quantum physics. (2) We develop a formalism for wave-based logic circuits with phase encoding. We motivate the formalism using the example of spin-wave circuits, and then demonstrate its utility in design, analysis and optimisation of Boolean logic circuits. (3) We investigate the elimination of inter-language grammatical bureaucracy in the distributional compositional circuits (DisCoCirc) framework. In particular, we develop a hybrid grammar for a restricted fragment of the Urdu language, and show that Urdu text endowed with this hybrid grammar maps surjectively to DisCoCirc text circuits. Furthermore, we show that for the same language fragment, Urdu and English text circuits become the same up to gate-level translation. The aforementioned work supports the view that a process-relational outlook in science is well-supported by applied category-theoretic tools, particularly string diagrams.
summary: This thesis investigates how string diagrams from applied category theory can serve as a universal language for modeling processes in quantum physics, computing, and linguistics. It successfully applies this framework to formalize constructor theory, design wave-based logic circuits, and unify the grammatical structures of different natural languages.
main_problem: The paper explores the utility of string diagrams within applied category theory as a unified mathematical tool for modeling processes across quantum foundations, quantum computing, and natural language processing.
main_result: The author demonstrates that string diagrams can formalize Constructor Theory as a process theory, develop a formalism for wave-based logic circuits, and reduce grammatical complexity in NLP by showing structural equivalence between English and Urdu text circuits.
method: The research employs symmetric monoidal categories and string diagrams to represent series and parallel compositions, utilizing diagrammatic rewriting rules and hybrid grammar development.
model_or_system: The mathematical framework is based on symmetric monoidal categories (process theories) and includes specific models such as Constructor Theory, wave-based logic circuits (inspired by spin-wave systems), and the DisCoCirc framework for linguistics.
important_parameters: The work considers regimes of phase encoding in wave-based circuits and restricted fragments of the Urdu language.
main_assumptions: The paper assumes the use of strict monoidal categories (via the Coherence Theorem) and focuses on restricted linguistic fragments.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate algebraic rewriting using string diagrams, showing how boxes (processes) can slide along wires, and demonstrate the topological equivalence of different algebraic strings.
paper_structure: The thesis progresses from a philosophical critique of atomism to the mathematical implementation of process theory, followed by specific applications in quantum foundations (Constructor Theory), quantum computing (wave-based logic), and NLP (DisCoCirc/Urdu grammar).
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, unified diagrammatic language (string diagrams) that can simplify complex algebraic transformations in quantum protocols, circuit optimization, and the study of quantum foundations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
